$schema_note: End-to-end scenarios narrating how the systems in rayflow_file_map.json's 'systems' object cooperate to accomplish something a real user or LLM agent cares about. This is a genuinely different kind of information from depends_on_systems/dependents_systems: those are mechanically aggregated from real imports (a script can compute them, and they're always exactly correct for the code as it stands). A scenario narrative is not derivable that way — 'why do these six systems, wired together this way, add up to a coherent capability' requires understanding intent, not just structure. It has to be written and kept current by an LLM that has actually read the code, not generated by any static analysis. There is deliberately no automated correctness check for these narratives (unlike file descriptions, which staleness_guard/stale_reminder.py can flag as touched); stale_reminder.py only hints when an edited file's system participates in a scenario's systems_path, as a nudge to re-read it, not a claim that it's wrong.

scenarios.title: A user runs a flow from the visual editor and watches it animate
scenarios.narrative: RunsPanel triggers a POST to /flows/{name}/run via the typed client in frontend-state's api.ts (the same single run endpoint any external API caller would use). For that to succeed the flow must be in rayflow.registry — the editor's POST /editor/flows/{name}/load (or an earlier run via /run, but /run itself no longer auto-loads: 'served' = 'explicitly loaded'). editor-api's routes.py hands off to execute_async, which dispatches execute on the engine system's FlowEngine. FlowEngine walks the BuiltFlow node by node, AWAITING every event push (node_start/edge_fire/node_done, plus the terminal flow_done/flow_error) into the RunQueue actor (state system) so the terminal event is never reordered ahead of the per-node ones the driver is waiting on. frontend-state's useRunStream hook consumes that SSE stream (reconnecting on drop via the run_id from run_start) and drives the animation state that frontend-canvas's FlowCanvas/NodeCard render. The user never sees any of this handoff — they just see nodes light up in execution order.

scenarios.title: An LLM agent authors, validates, and runs a flow through the MCP layer without touching the editor UI
scenarios.narrative: An external LLM client calls get_guide and list_nodes (mcp system) to learn the node vocabulary, drafts a FlowDef, and loops on validate_flow until build's validate_all() returns no errors (it collects every problem in one pass instead of raising on the first, precisely so an agent can fix several issues per round-trip instead of one). create_flow/update_flow reuse editor-api's storage.py directly (same process, same logic the visual editor's REST routes use — the MCP layer is a curated subset, not a reimplementation). Before run_flow/test_flow can execute, the flow must be registered in rayflow.registry: run_flow loads it on demand (via rayflow.api.load, which pre-validates + spawns actors) if it isn't already served; test_flow does the same. Once served, the engine system runs it exactly as the visual editor's /run does; the agent gets structured JSON back instead of watching an animation. The whole loop is designed to close in a handful of round-trips because validate_flow front-loads every error at once.

scenarios.title: A flow reacts automatically when another flow changes a watched variable
scenarios.narrative: Flow A's Set node writes a variable through the state system's GraphState actor, which only publishes to the events system's EventBroker if the value actually changed (comparing old vs new, resolving any ObjectRef first) — this is the single guard preventing write-storms from becoming trigger-storms. If Flow B is registered as watching that variable (via serve_events, which also marked it watched on Flow A's GraphState at registration time), the publish fires a Ray task that runs in a *different worker process* than the driver, resolves Flow B's engine/queue by their detached actor names (this worker's process-local registry is empty, so name lookup is the only option), and calls execute() on Flow B's engine directly. Flow B's OnVariableChange node receives value/old as if they were normal flow inputs. Two flows, zero shared memory, connected only by a string-matched event name.

scenarios.title: A user edits a custom node's source in the browser and the next run uses the new code, with no server restart
scenarios.narrative: CustomNodesPanel's CodeMirror editor sends the edited source via PUT /editor/custom-nodes/{name}/source. editor-api's custom_nodes_routes.py runs ast.parse() first — purely a syntax gate, it doesn't execute anything — writes the file only if that succeeds, then forces a hot reload: it evicts any custom_nodes.* modules cached in sys.modules (so the next import re-reads the file instead of serving a stale bytecode object) and calls reset_catalog() + get_catalog() to rebuild the nodes system's NodeCatalog from scratch. There's no process restart and no explicit signal to the frontend that anything changed — the next flow run simply resolves the node type against the freshly rebuilt catalog.

scenarios.title: A flow embeds another flow as a reusable subflow, and the engine never finds out
scenarios.narrative: A flow can reference another saved flow via a CallFlow node — authored through either editor-api or mcp, both just producing a FlowDef against the schema system's models. All of the subflow handling happens once, at build time, entirely inside the build system: flatten() splices the sub-flow's nodes into the parent's namespace with ids prefixed like parent/sub/node, reparenting every exec and data reference (_reparent_exec_in, _reparent_inputs) so they point at the new prefixed ids. By the time a BuiltFlow reaches the engine system, CallFlow boundaries no longer exist as a runtime concept — FlowEngine executes one flat graph and has no idea nesting was ever involved. This is why 'one node = one Python class' and 'flat namespace' are both true at once: nesting is a build-time illusion, not a runtime one.
